chapters.title: The bag man: the crime world's accountant
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered who the best criminal would be? You might picture someone constantly outsmarting the authorities, staying one step ahead at all times. While accurate, the most successful criminal isn't always on the opposite side of the law. Often, it's someone deeply embedded within the system, maybe even as high up as a president — no need to evade or run; they're already several steps ahead.

A bag man is, by dictionary definition, an individual or agent who collects and distributes the proceeds from illegal activities. In every illicit operation, one person is responsible for holding the bag discreetly and distributing the funds fairly while keeping a portion for themselves.

A discrete existence is essential in the world of crime, especially if you lead an outside life to avoid suspicion.

It seems implausible that someone as prominent and visible as the Vice President of the United States could be a member of a crime syndicate, let alone its bag man. Yet, this was the reality with Spiro Agnew. While many were unaware, a select few possessed enough intel to bring him down.

At the peak of his career, Agnew had the ambition, political clout, and connections to ascend to the presidency — with Nixon's blessing, of course. Despite having enough influence and support, including from future President George Bush, to shake off the accusations, Agnew conceded to fervent legal efforts, ultimately retiring and serving a reduced sentence.

If the emerging public image of Agnew was as a singularly divisive national figure, he embraced it. ~ Rachel Maddow, Michael Yarvitz

chapters.title: How to get away with laundering
chapters.text:
The greatest mystery is how a serving vice president successfully ran a bribery and extortion ring undetected. To understand, we must first go back to when it all started. From 1962 to 1966, Agnew, then the County Executive in Baltimore, slowly built his criminal empire, cultivating favors from influential people as insurance.

During this time as Baltimore County Executive, he indulged in illicit contracts and took wads of cash as “kickbacks” from contractors, a practice that persisted into his vice presidential role. He didn't limit himself to conventional fraud; instead, he exploited his position, leveraging power to evade accountability.

Given the events involving former President Donald Trump, it isn't far-fetched to think that criminal activity could occur in the White House. For example, consider the 2016 allegations suggesting that Trump stole the election with the help of Russia.

As the Agnew investigation revealed, many illicit activities go unnoticed even at the helm of government.

This situation prompts a critical question: are there sufficient checks on government officials, and what prevents them from being involved in criminal activities? When the barrage of accusations and subsequent investigations of Agnew began, he used the might of his position to push them into near remission. It highlighted a concerning reality that government officials can shield individuals from the scrutinizing eyes of the law.

Funny thing this, old habits die hard. And when the exercise of those habits has delivered fame and glory and raucous applause, they are unlikely to die at all. ~ Rachel Maddow, Michael Yarvitz

chapters.title: Operating a criminal empire from the White House
chapters.text:
Like Trump, Agnew was also a Republican. He relied heavily on strong-arming the opposition to achieve dominance and ward off any suspicions or accusations from the media. Even if allegations did arise, both Agnew and Trump would pin them on “the left” or some other assumed attempt to tarnish their image. Like many right-wing politicians, Agnew manipulated public opinion by claiming that his accusations were based solely on differences of opinion.

Agnew evaded any serious prosecution by feigning innocence and skillfully shifting blame, effectively avoiding intense scrutiny. Charging him would have required a bold move at the time, given his formidable political standing. He was a fierce opponent supported by a network of loyalists in government, who successfully navigated the accusations and maintained his power.

Criminals can evade the law because they have frequently built reliable support systems within law enforcement.

The only difference between having a successful political career and a thriving criminal organization is that only one allows you to pose for pictures with the press as a hero. Agnew managed his political side and infamous alter ego through manipulation and bribery. He was so far out of reach that when he came forward to plead guilty, he only admitted to tax fraud and kept his real crimes hidden until now.

Did you know? Agnew became the second Vice President in US history to resign and the first to do so because of scandal. Despite admitting to tax evasion, he demanded that the courts drop the corruption charges against him. This case happened less than a year before Nixon's resignation, and Agnew was fined $10,000 with a three-year probation.

chapters.title: Prosecuting the most powerful man in the country
chapters.text:
In a twist of fate, Agnew, then Governor of Maryland, unwittingly appointed the attorney who would bring his political career to an end. At the time, the young attorney, George Beall, was appointed to the Maryland Criminal Compensation Board.

It is important to note that there are good people in law enforcement who are committed to maintaining law and order.

When Beall became a US attorney in 1970, he worked on petty investigations that eventually grew into larger targets — the top dogs in the crime world. But soon enough, George Beall set his sights on Agnew. At first, the probe concentrated on corrupt officials in Baltimore County, but it eventually circled back to Agnew.

Beall's investigation dug deep into the corrupt business deals, officials, and all major players involved in this crime ring. He was committed to getting to the root of the matter and kept pushing until one of the contractors he questioned, Lester Matz, named Agnew as a steady recipient of kickbacks in exchange for contracts for “years.”

Agnew's extortion spanned his tenure as County Executive, Governor, and Vice President. Despite the investigation gaining momentum and more witnesses coming forward, Agnew refused to budge. It wasn't until rumors surfaced that President Nixon had asked him to step down twice that he finally complied.

chapters.title: The government must check itself
chapters.text:
The investigation and eventual prosecution of Vice President Agnew illustrate how little we know about the inner workings of government. Many other illicit activities slip under the radar because of concealment, as in the case of Agnew, or out of fear. That's why it's important to hold public figures accountable.

Regardless of a person's position or power, no one has a right to evade punishment. Implementing a well-deserved punishment will also decrease the chances of it repeating. When government officials realize they are not above the law, they will think twice before following the example of a bag man. By making such changes, the people will feel safer and more confident in their government since their officials must follow the same laws as them.

Crime does not restrict itself to the dark alleys and slums; it can exist anywhere.

According to Maddow and Yarvitz, much like Agnew, the Trump administration relied on intimidation and scare tactics to discourage anyone from investigating their lives and opposing their opinions. Journalists, prosecutors, and even law enforcement might be deterred from coming forward with this “dirt” even today.

The government must set up intentional checks on its activities and members to address this. Though this system would require regular polling of government officials and their accountability for actions that lead to or could lead to crime, it has its merits.

After all, Agnew's rap sheet was so long it dated farther back than his time as a Governor, yet he kept it hidden. Even during campaign season, he averted suspicions and won, albeit indirectly, alongside Nixon. A stricter check on government officials is imperative to mitigate these risks and promote openness and accountability.

chapters.title: There is a way to prepare for discrepancies in the government
chapters.text:
It is imperative for the government to proactively address internal issues to prevent scandals like the Agnew incident. Rather than waiting for media scrutiny or fostering secrecy, the government must take responsibility. However, as highlighted by the Agnew case, this plan may be challenging to implement without knowing how many criminals may be hiding behind the curtain. Furthermore, we cannot expect the government to be completely trustworthy and manage itself.

Agnew's story shows how easily government officials can conceal a massive crime network. How can we be sure that those responsible for ensuring the clarity of the government’s actions are not also corrupt? The autonomous self-managing check-up system might sound great in theory, but it's likely to fail in practice.

Therefore, the government needs an independent external office that monitors its activities, checks their legitimacy, and punishes those who violate the law.

By calling on the government to scrutinize its officials personally, crimes will just be better hidden.

Officials can hide their nefarious activities by letting the government control investigations and prosecutions. This action refers to finding a means to stifle the media, as evidenced in historical cases like Agnew's and more recent instances during the Trump Administration.

A show of force, combined with the ease with which criminals can manipulate the media, has made it much easier for criminals in government to twist the truth. Agnew was able to bully the media and law enforcement into easing up on their investigations. To counteract this, it would be a good idea to create a system to minimize the chances of a government body manipulating or even threatening the media to portray a particular image.

Even today, this trend continues to thrive. And even though we want the government to do better and hold itself accountable, we should be aware that this autonomy can be dangerous and lead to even worse covert criminal activities. So, to prevent officials from pushing hidden agendas, the government should collaborate with external organizations to provide proper oversight and prevent covert illegal activities.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Bag Man recounts how a serving US Vice President stepped into the muddy waters of criminal activity while in office. From his time as a County Executive, Agnew exploited the corrupt system for personal gain. He forced contractors to come to him for jobs that would pay him a portion of returns as kickbacks. These kickbacks served as payment for getting them jobs that may have rightfully been theirs had they followed the proper channels.

As Agnew ascended to Governor of Maryland and then the White House, his extortion and bribery persisted as his political career and influence grew. With an intricate and powerful network of loyalists willing to fight for his interests in his corner, he was virtually untouchable.

That was until George Beall, a lawyer Agnew had previously appointed as part of a criminal compensation board, rose to State Attorney and aimed to rid his state of criminals and illegal activity. He primarily focused on the corruption involved in awarding contracts and their illegality. As the brains behind the operation, Agnew had Beall knocking on his door in no time until he resigned and pleaded guilty.

Try this

If you are offered or asked to offer a bribe by a member of law enforcement, refuse it. While it might seem easier to comply, giving in will only encourage further systemic corruption. Be stern, don’t listen to the threats, and report the incident to the authorities. Remember that no matter how small, your actions and choices matter and will contribute to transforming the unjust system.

Read it till the end?
